preamble: Prothesis or epenthesis of [h] involves the insertion of a glottal fricative before a vowel-initial word or syllable. This can occur as a grammatical marker (as in Celtic languages), as a way to avoid hiatus, or as a hypercorrection in dialects where [h] is regularly dropped (such as Cockney English). It is also seen in the historical development of Ancient Greek.

examples.note: In some cases, initial vowels in Greek acquired a 'rough breathing' (h-), though this often reflects a lost *s- or *y-.
Grammatical h-prothesis: the 3rd person feminine possessive 'a' (her) triggers the insertion of [h] before a vowel-initial noun.
Hypercorrection: speakers of H-dropping dialects may insert [h] where it does not historically belong, especially when trying to speak formally.

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Sihler, A. L. (1995). New Comparative Grammar of Greek and Latin.
